text:
a) The apparatus and chemicals used in the demonstration of osmosis may include:
- A semipermeable membrane, such as a dialysis tubing or a visking tubing
- A beaker or a container to hold the solution
- Distilled water
- A solute, such as salt (sodium chloride) or sugar
- A potato or an eggplant to demonstrate osmosis in a living tissue
- A balance to measure the initial and final mass of the potato or eggplant
- A stopwatch or a timer to measure the time elapsed during the experiment
- A ruler or a measuring tape to measure the initial and final length of the potato or eggplant
- A marker or a pen to label the beakers and the potato or eggplant

b) A control that might be used in this activity is to have a separate beaker with distilled water and a potato or an eggplant, without any solute added. This control will help to compare the results of the experiment with a situation where no osmosis occurs, as the water has the same concentration as the potato or eggplant.

c) The result(s) of the demonstration may include:
- A change in the mass of the potato or eggplant after being placed in the solution
- A change in the length of the potato or eggplant after being placed in the solution
- The potato or eggplant will gain mass and length if the solution is hypotonic (less concentrated) compared to the cell sap
- The potato or eggplant will lose mass and shrink if the solution is hypertonic (more concentrated) compared to the cell sap
- No significant change in mass and length if the solution is isotonic (same concentration) as the cell sap

d) The result(s) can be explained by the process of osmosis, where water molecules move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration through a semipermeable membrane. If the solution is hypotonic, water molecules will move into the potato or eggplant, causing it to gain mass and length. If the solution is hypertonic, water molecules will move out of the potato or eggplant, causing it to lose mass and shrink. If the solution is isotonic, there will be no net movement of water molecules, resulting in no significant change in mass and length.

e) Storing food in salty water prevents the growth of bacteria or fungi on the stored food because the high salt concentration creates a hypertonic environment that inhibits the growth of microorganisms. The salt draws water out of the bacterial or fungal cells, causing them to shrink and eventually die. This method is commonly used to preserve food, such as pickling vegetables or curing meats.

f) Apart from storing food in salty water, other ways to prevent food spoilage include:
- Refrigeration: keeping food at a low temperature to slow down the growth of microorganisms
- Freezing: keeping food at a very low temperature to stop the growth of microorganisms
- Dehydration: removing the water content from food to prevent the growth of microorganisms
- Canning: heating food to a high temperature to kill microorganisms and then sealing it in airtight containers
- Irradiation: exposing food to ionizing radiation to kill microorganisms
- Vacuum packaging: removing the air from food packaging to prevent the growth of microorganisms
- Adding preservatives: adding chemicals to food to prevent the growth of microorganisms.
